sections.plain_summary: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (hEDS) and hypermobility spectrum disorder (HSD) are connective tissue conditions in which the body's collagen — the structural protein holding joints, skin, blood vessels, and organs in place — does not work as it should. This causes joints that move too far, pain that can be widespread, fatigue, and a wide range of symptoms across multiple body systems. Because collagen is everywhere in the body, these conditions can affect almost any organ. They are frequently missed or misdiagnosed for years, and many people with hEDS or HSD are told their symptoms are anxiety, attention-seeking, or simply unexplained.
sections.detailed_summary:
Hypermobile EDS (hEDS) and hypermobility spectrum disorder (HSD) sit on the same clinical spectrum of heritable connective tissue hypermobility. hEDS is diagnosed against a strict 2017 international criteria set — requiring joint hypermobility with a high Beighton score, plus features such as skin changes, musculoskeletal complications, and a relevant family history. HSD is the category applied when hypermobility causes genuine symptoms and functional impairment but the full hEDS criteria are not met. The two conditions are managed very similarly and share most comorbidities.

The underlying problem is thought to involve faulty or insufficient collagen and related extracellular matrix proteins, though the specific gene or genes responsible for hEDS have not yet been identified. This distinguishes it from other EDS subtypes (such as classical or vascular EDS), which have known genetic causes and are tested by gene panel. Because connective tissue is structural to every organ system, the consequences of this defect extend well beyond joints: the gut wall, autonomic nerves, mast cells, pelvic floor, dura, and blood vessel walls are all affected in varying degrees.

People with hEDS/HSD frequently experience years or even decades of symptoms across multiple specialties — orthopaedics, gastroenterology, cardiology, gynaecology, urology, and neurology — without a unifying diagnosis. The average diagnostic delay is reported at over a decade. The condition is heavily female-predominant in clinical populations, though this may partly reflect diagnostic bias. There is a significant and growing evidence base linking hEDS/HSD to dysautonomia (especially POTS), mast cell activation syndrome (MCAS), functional gastrointestinal disorders, chronic pain with central sensitisation, and neurodivergence. This cluster — sometimes called the 'hypermobility triad' or informally the 'trifecta' — appears with striking frequency and may share common mechanisms.

prevalence.summary: More common than previously thought, affecting an estimated 1 in 500 to 1 in 5,000 people, with significant underdiagnosis.
prevalence.estimate: 0.02–0.2% for hEDS; HSD broader and likely higher; joint hypermobility alone affects 10–20% of the population
prevalence.notes: Prevalence estimates vary widely because diagnostic criteria have changed over time, and the boundary between asymptomatic hypermobility and HSD is clinically contested. Female individuals are diagnosed 3–5 times more often than male individuals in most clinical series, though whether this reflects true sex difference or diagnostic bias is debated. Diagnosis is heavily dependent on access to informed clinicians, creating significant geographic and socioeconomic disparity in identification.

subgroups.name: hEDS (Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome)
subgroups.description: Meets the full 2017 international diagnostic criteria: generalised joint hypermobility with Beighton score ≥5 (adult), plus at least two of three feature clusters (musculoskeletal complications, connective tissue features such as soft skin or striae, and positive family history). No causative gene identified.
distinguishing_features: Beighton score typically ≥5/9 in adults
Often has a relevant family history
Skin features such as soft/velvety texture, easy bruising, or stretch marks disproportionate to age or BMI
Musculoskeletal complications: recurrent dislocations, subluxations, chronic joint pain
Diagnosed clinically — no genetic test available

subgroups.name: HSD (Hypermobility Spectrum Disorder)
subgroups.description: Hypermobility causes symptomatic impairment but full hEDS criteria are not met. Includes generalised HSD (G-HSD), peripheral HSD (P-HSD), localised HSD (L-HSD), and historical HSD (for those who were hypermobile but have stiffened with age or injury).
distinguishing_features: Joint hypermobility is present but may not meet full Beighton threshold
Symptoms are real and functionally limiting — not a milder or less legitimate condition
Managed almost identically to hEDS
Comorbidities (POTS, MCAS, dysautonomia) occur similarly to hEDS

subgroups.name: Predominantly pain-dominant presentation
subgroups.description: Some individuals have severe, widespread chronic pain as their primary burden, often overlapping with fibromyalgia or central sensitisation, with joint instability as a secondary feature.
distinguishing_features: Widespread allodynia and hyperalgesia
Central sensitisation features: pain disproportionate to injury, widespread tender points
Often also meets fibromyalgia criteria
May have relatively modest joint laxity scores despite severe pain

subgroups.name: Predominantly dysautonomia-dominant presentation
subgroups.description: Autonomic nervous system dysfunction is the primary driver of symptoms, with POTS, orthostatic intolerance, and fatigue dominating the picture.
distinguishing_features: Postural tachycardia, presyncope, or syncope
Severe fatigue and post-exertional malaise
Brain fog, cognitive difficulties
Orthostatic intolerance as a daily limiting factor

subgroups.name: Predominantly gastrointestinal presentation
subgroups.description: GI symptoms — nausea, bloating, early satiety, constipation, diarrhoea, or gastroparesis — dominate the clinical picture, often leading to GI-first evaluation.
distinguishing_features: Slow gastric emptying or gastroparesis
Functional GI symptoms including IBS-like pattern
Pelvic floor dysfunction contributing to evacuation difficulty
Mast cell activation may drive food reactivity and gut inflammation

subgroups.name: Paediatric and adolescent onset
subgroups.description: hEDS/HSD frequently presents in childhood or adolescence, often with growing pains, recurrent sprains, fatigue, and school attendance difficulties, but is often not identified until adulthood.
distinguishing_features: Higher Beighton scores are normal in children, requiring adjusted thresholds
May present as persistent joint pain, clumsiness, or 'growing pains'
School avoidance and fatigue are common presenting features
Onset or worsening at puberty is frequently reported, particularly in girls

symptoms.symptom: Joint hypermobility — joints that move beyond normal range
symptoms.notes: Classic features include ability to hyperextend elbows, knees, and fingers; touching thumbs to forearms; palms flat on floor with straight knees. Beighton scale is used clinically. Hypermobility may reduce with age and injury.
Joints partially or fully dislocate with minimal provocation — sometimes during sleep or routine movement. Shoulders, hips, kneecaps, and fingers are most commonly affected.
Pain is often multi-site, fluctuating, and disproportionate to visible tissue damage. Frequently misdiagnosed as fibromyalgia, psychosomatic, or medically unexplained pain.
Fatigue in hEDS/HSD is often profound and not relieved by rest. May be driven by the physical effort of stabilising hypermobile joints, autonomic dysfunction, poor sleep, or comorbid MCAS.
A worsening of symptoms — pain, fatigue, brain fog — in the 12–48 hours following physical or cognitive exertion. Common in those with comorbid dysautonomia or MCAS.
Difficulty with memory, word retrieval, concentration, and processing speed. Often worsened by orthostatic intolerance (reduced cerebral blood flow when upright) and poor sleep.
Lightheadedness, presyncope, palpitations, or worsening brain fog when standing upright. Reflects autonomic dysfunction (dysautonomia) and is common in hEDS/HSD.
Often a feature of POTS — heart rate rises excessively on standing. Can be alarming and frequently leads to cardiac investigations that return normal.
Can be related to gastroparesis (delayed gastric emptying), POTS, MCAS, or vagal nerve dysfunction. Often worsened in the morning or when upright.
Hypermobile gut wall may allow excessive gas accumulation. SIBO, MCAS, and slow motility can all contribute to bloating in this population.
Altered gut motility — either too slow or dysregulated — is common. An IBS diagnosis is frequently made before hEDS is identified. Both constipation and diarrhoea can alternate.
Difficulty swallowing or sensation of food sticking, due to lax oesophageal tissue and abnormal motility. May be investigated by gastroenterologists without identifying hEDS as the root cause.
Skin may be unusually soft and smooth to the touch. It may stretch further than normal but usually recoils. Not always present and not required for HSD diagnosis.

symptoms.symptom: Easy bruising and slow wound healing
symptoms.notes: Fragile blood vessels and connective tissue make bruising more likely with minor trauma. Wounds may take longer to heal and scars may be atrophic (paper-thin) or stretched.

symptoms.symptom: Stretch marks disproportionate to age, weight, or growth
symptoms.notes: Striae appearing in areas or at ages where they would not be expected — for example, on the back or thighs in young, slim individuals — can be a connective tissue feature.
Both tension-type and migrainous headaches are increased in hEDS/HSD. Craniocervical instability (CCI) and intracranial pressure dysregulation may contribute in some individuals.

symptoms.symptom: Anxiety, panic attacks, and autonomic hyperarousal
symptoms.notes: The physical symptoms of dysautonomia (racing heart, breathlessness, presyncope) mimic anxiety and can trigger panic responses. True anxiety disorders are also more prevalent. It is important not to reduce all anxiety in this population to psychological cause.

symptoms.symptom: Bladder urgency, frequency, or pelvic pain
symptoms.notes: Pelvic floor laxity and connective tissue fragility can cause bladder prolapse, urgency incontinence, interstitial cystitis-like symptoms, and incomplete emptying.

symptoms.symptom: Heavy or painful periods and endometriosis
symptoms.notes: Menorrhagia (heavy periods) is more common. There is an association between hEDS/HSD and endometriosis, though the mechanism is not fully established.
Difficulty regulating body temperature — feeling too hot or too cold without clear cause. May reflect autonomic nervous system dysfunction.
Many individuals react to a wide range of foods. This may reflect MCAS, histamine intolerance, underlying gut motility issues causing SIBO, or central sensitisation amplifying gut discomfort.

symptoms.symptom: Widespread skin reactivity, flushing, and hives
symptoms.notes: Mast cell activation (MCAS) — a common comorbidity — causes episodes of flushing, urticaria, itching, and anaphylaxis-like reactions. Skin is frequently the first organ affected.
Pain, autonomic hyperarousal, and the physical discomfort of hypermobile joints make sleep difficult. Many people report waking unrefreshed regardless of hours slept.
The sensory feedback from hypermobile joints is often impaired, leading to difficulty knowing where limbs are in space, frequent stumbling, and difficulty with fine motor tasks.
Temporomandibular joint dysfunction is common in hEDS/HSD. Teeth may also be more fragile, with higher rates of cavities and enamel defects in some individuals.

key_patterns: High Beighton score (≥5/9 in adults, ≥6/9 in children under 16) with symptomatic joint complaints across multiple sites
Multi-system symptom history involving joints, gut, autonomic, and skin — across multiple specialties without a unifying diagnosis
Symptom onset or worsening in adolescence, often around puberty
Strong family history of hypermobility, chronic pain, or unexplained multi-system illness
Comorbid POTS confirmed by active stand test or tilt table: heart rate rise ≥30 bpm on standing
Recurrent subluxations or dislocations documented across multiple joints
Food and chemical reactivity pattern consistent with MCAS alongside musculoskeletal and autonomic features
Previous diagnoses of IBS, fibromyalgia, chronic fatigue syndrome, or functional neurological disorder in someone with joint laxity

at_home.name: Beighton Score Self-Assessment
at_home.description: The Beighton scale includes nine simple manoeuvres: little finger hyperextension past 90° (1 point each hand), thumb touching forearm (1 point each hand), elbow hyperextension past 10° (1 point each arm), knee hyperextension past 10° (1 point each leg), and palms flat on floor with straight knees (1 point). Score of 5 or more suggests generalised joint hypermobility in adults. Many online video guides demonstrate the positions correctly.
at_home.safety_notes: Beighton score is a screening tool only — a low score does not rule out hypermobility or hEDS, particularly in older individuals whose joints have stiffened over time. Do not force joints; gentle assessment only.

at_home.name: Active Stand Test (poor man's tilt table)
at_home.description: Lie flat for 10 minutes, then stand and measure heart rate immediately on standing and at 2, 5, and 10 minutes. A rise of 30 bpm or more (or to above 120 bpm) within 10 minutes of standing, in the absence of significant blood pressure drop, is consistent with POTS. A smartwatch or pulse oximeter can be used.
at_home.safety_notes: Stop if feeling faint, very dizzy, or unwell. Have someone present. This is a screening indicator only — clinical diagnosis of POTS requires formal testing.

at_home.name: Symptom and body system diary
at_home.description: Tracking symptoms across body systems — joints, gut, energy, cognition, autonomic, skin — over 4–8 weeks often reveals patterns (e.g. worsening around menstrual cycle, after exertion, or in certain positions) that are useful for clinical appointments and for recognising the multi-system nature of the condition.
at_home.safety_notes: No physical risk. Emotionally difficult for some — recognising the extent of symptoms can be confronting. Useful to share with practitioners who may otherwise see only one symptom cluster.

at_home.name: Positional symptom tracking
at_home.description: Noting whether symptoms (dizziness, brain fog, nausea, palpitations) are worse when standing or sitting upright versus lying down is a practical way to identify orthostatic intolerance at home.
at_home.safety_notes: None, but if symptoms are severe, medical evaluation for POTS or other autonomic conditions is warranted.

clinical.name: Clinical diagnosis against 2017 hEDS criteria
clinical.description: hEDS is diagnosed by a clinician applying the 2017 international diagnostic criteria: Beighton score, feature cluster checklist, and exclusion of other heritable connective tissue disorders. No blood or genetic test confirms hEDS. The assessment requires physical examination and detailed history.
clinical.availability: Specialist rheumatologists, geneticists, and some physiotherapists or sports medicine doctors with EDS expertise. Access is limited in many regions — waiting lists are long and knowledgeable clinicians are scarce.

clinical.name: EDS genetic panel (for other EDS subtypes)
clinical.description: A panel of genes associated with classical, vascular, kyphoscoliotic, and other EDS subtypes. Used to rule out genetically defined EDS types. hEDS has no known gene and is NOT identified by genetic testing.
clinical.availability: Ordered by geneticists or specialists. Relevant when vascular EDS or other subtypes are suspected. A negative panel result does not rule out hEDS or HSD.

clinical.name: Tilt table test
clinical.description: Formal assessment for POTS and other orthostatic conditions. The patient is tilted from lying to upright on a motorised table while heart rate and blood pressure are continuously monitored. Diagnoses POTS (HR rise ≥30 bpm without significant hypotension) and neurally mediated syncope.
clinical.availability: Cardiology or autonomic neurology departments. Waiting times can be long; active stand test is often used as a clinic alternative.

clinical.name: Gastric emptying study (scintigraphy)
clinical.description: A nuclear medicine test using a radiolabelled meal to measure how quickly food moves through the stomach. Diagnoses gastroparesis — significantly delayed gastric emptying — which is more common in hEDS/HSD than in the general population.
clinical.availability: Ordered by gastroenterologists. Standard in most hospital-based nuclear medicine departments.

clinical.name: MCAS workup (serum tryptase, urine prostaglandins, urine histamine metabolites)
clinical.description: Serum baseline tryptase and a panel of mediators measured during or shortly after a reaction episode. MCAS diagnosis also requires symptom criteria and response to mast cell-targeting treatments. Normal results do not rule out MCAS — testing sensitivity is limited.
clinical.availability: Ordered by allergists, immunologists, or haematologists. Many practitioners are not familiar with MCAS criteria; specialist centres are preferred.

clinical.name: Echocardiogram
clinical.description: Cardiac ultrasound to assess for aortic root dilatation, mitral valve prolapse, and other structural cardiac findings. Mitral valve prolapse is more common in EDS. Aortic root measurement is important to exclude vascular EDS and related conditions.
clinical.availability: Ordered by cardiologists or GPs. Standard imaging.

clinical.name: Pelvic floor physiotherapy assessment
clinical.description: Internal and external assessment by a specialised pelvic floor physiotherapist. Identifies prolapse, hypertonic or hypotonic pelvic floor, and dysfunction patterns relevant to bladder, bowel, and pelvic pain symptoms.
clinical.availability: Pelvic health physiotherapists in most regions. Referral from GP or specialist, or direct access in some countries.

clinical.name: Neuropsychological assessment for ADHD and autism
clinical.description: Formal cognitive and behavioural assessment for attention difficulties and autistic traits. Both ADHD and autism are significantly overrepresented in hEDS/HSD populations.
clinical.availability: Psychiatrists, psychologists, or specialist neurodevelopmental services. Waiting times are often very long.

related_conditions.name: POTS / dysautonomia
related_conditions.explanation: POTS (postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome) occurs in an estimated 30–80% of people with hEDS/HSD depending on the sample. Lax blood vessel walls and autonomic neuropathy — both connected to collagen dysfunction — impair venous return and autonomic regulation when standing. The hEDS-POTS-MCAS triad is well described in the literature.

related_conditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome
related_conditions.explanation: MCAS co-occurs with hEDS/HSD at rates far above the general population — estimates range from 25–70% in specialist samples. Mast cells are structurally embedded in connective tissue, and disrupted extracellular matrix may lead to mast cell dysregulation. MCAS drives food reactivity, flushing, gut symptoms, and anaphylactoid episodes in this population.

related_conditions.name: IBS
related_conditions.explanation: IBS is frequently diagnosed in people with hEDS/HSD before the underlying connective tissue disorder is identified. Gut hypermotility, pelvic floor laxity, slow gastric emptying, and MCAS all produce IBS-like symptoms. Treating IBS symptomatically without identifying hEDS as a driver often produces incomplete or temporary relief.

related_conditions.name: Gastroparesis
related_conditions.explanation: Delayed gastric emptying is significantly more common in hEDS/HSD than in the general population, thought to result from lax gastric smooth muscle connective tissue and autonomic neuropathy affecting gastric motility. Presents as nausea, early satiety, bloating, and vomiting.

related_conditions.name: Fibromyalgia
related_conditions.explanation: The widespread pain, fatigue, and cognitive symptoms of hEDS/HSD overlap significantly with fibromyalgia criteria, and many individuals receive a fibromyalgia diagnosis before hEDS is identified. Fibromyalgia may also be a genuine comorbidity reflecting central sensitisation that develops secondary to years of undertreated joint pain.

related_conditions.name: Central sensitisation
related_conditions.explanation: Chronic, undertreated pain in hEDS/HSD can lead to central sensitisation — a state in which the central nervous system amplifies pain signals, causing pain that is disproportionate to tissue damage. This is a real neurological consequence of long-term pain, not a psychological explanation.

related_conditions.name: Anxiety disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Anxiety disorders are more prevalent in hEDS/HSD. This may partly reflect shared neurobiology, partly the physical symptoms of dysautonomia being interpreted as anxiety, and partly the psychological impact of chronic illness and medical gaslighting. Distinguishing somatic anxiety symptoms from dysautonomia is clinically important.

related_conditions.name: ADHD
related_conditions.explanation: ADHD is significantly overrepresented in hEDS/HSD — studies report rates 3–5 times higher than expected. The mechanism is not fully established; shared neurodevelopmental pathways and noradrenaline dysregulation have been proposed. Many people receive an ADHD diagnosis around the same time as EDS.

related_conditions.name: Endometriosis
related_conditions.explanation: Endometriosis co-occurs with hEDS/HSD more frequently than would be expected by chance. Connective tissue fragility, immune dysregulation, and mast cell involvement may contribute. The combination creates a high pelvic pain burden and diagnostic complexity.

related_conditions.name: Pelvic floor dysfunction
related_conditions.explanation: Lax connective tissue in the pelvic floor leads to prolapse, poor muscular support, and dysfunction in bladder, bowel, and sexual function. Pelvic floor issues are very common in hEDS/HSD and are often managed by gynaecologists or urologists without the underlying connective tissue diagnosis.

related_conditions.name: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO)
related_conditions.explanation: Slow or dysregulated gut motility in hEDS/HSD creates conditions that favour SIBO. Some studies report elevated SIBO prevalence in hypermobility populations. SIBO produces bloating, altered bowel habits, and abdominal pain that compounds the gut symptoms of hEDS/HSD.

related_conditions.name: Histamine intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Histamine intolerance can occur independently of MCAS or alongside it. Impaired DAO enzyme activity — sometimes found in hEDS/HSD — reduces the ability to break down dietary histamine, causing flushing, headache, gut symptoms, and skin reactions after high-histamine foods.

related_conditions.name: Functional dyspepsia
related_conditions.explanation: Upper GI symptoms — nausea, early satiety, bloating, upper abdominal discomfort — in hEDS/HSD are often labelled functional dyspepsia. Gastroparesis, oesophageal dysmotility, and MCAS are frequently the actual drivers in this population.

related_conditions.name: Depression
related_conditions.explanation: Depression is more prevalent in hEDS/HSD, in part as a consequence of chronic pain, fatigue, medical invalidation, and loss of function. Neurobiological mechanisms may also be shared. Depression compounds pain sensitivity and reduces engagement with physical management strategies.

related_conditions.name: Trauma / PTSD
related_conditions.explanation: Medical trauma — from years of dismissed symptoms, invasive investigations, and being told symptoms are psychological — is common in hEDS/HSD. There is also emerging evidence of elevated rates of developmental trauma in the EDS population, though causality is not established.

related_conditions.name: Coeliac disease
related_conditions.explanation: Coeliac disease can mimic the gastrointestinal and fatigue symptoms of hEDS/HSD, and the two can co-occur. Coeliac should be excluded in those with significant GI symptoms before attributing everything to hEDS.

related_conditions.name: Craniocervical instability (CCI)
related_conditions.explanation: Ligamentous laxity at the craniocervical junction can lead to instability in the upper cervical spine in hEDS/HSD. This may contribute to headache, neck pain, dizziness, dysautonomia, and neurological symptoms. CCI is increasingly recognised in the EDS community but remains controversial in mainstream neurology.

related_conditions.name: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD)
related_conditions.explanation: Multiple studies and clinical observations have found elevated rates of autism in hEDS/HSD populations and vice versa. Proposed mechanisms include shared sensory processing differences, connective tissue effects on the nervous system, and common genetic influences. The overlap is striking enough to warrant systematic screening in both directions.

related_conditions.name: Chronic fatigue syndrome / ME
related_conditions.explanation: ME/CFS and hEDS/HSD share significant symptom overlap — fatigue, post-exertional malaise, cognitive difficulties, autonomic dysfunction — and commonly co-occur. Some researchers believe a subset of ME/CFS cases represent undiagnosed hEDS with secondary dysautonomia. Misdiagnosis in both directions is common.

related_conditions.name: Intracranial hypertension / hypotension
related_conditions.explanation: Both intracranial hypertension (raised pressure, causing headache, visual changes, pulsatile tinnitus) and spontaneous intracranial hypotension (from CSF leaks due to dural fragility) are reported more frequently in hEDS/HSD. Dural fragility from collagen defects is thought to be the mechanism for CSF leaks.

strategies.name: Physiotherapy with EDS-informed approach
strategies.description: Targeted exercise to build muscular support around hypermobile joints — reducing subluxations, pain, and fatigue. Focuses on proprioception training, strength, and joint stability rather than flexibility. This is the single most evidence-supported management tool for hEDS/HSD. Standard physiotherapy that stretches already-hypermobile joints can cause harm.
strategies.notes: Must be with a physiotherapist who understands EDS. Stretching-based yoga, standard flexibility training, or approaches that push range of motion may worsen instability and pain. Progress is often slow and pacing is essential.

strategies.name: Pacing and energy management
strategies.description: Structuring daily activity to avoid post-exertional crashes. This involves identifying an 'energy envelope', building in planned rest, and avoiding boom-bust cycles where high-activity periods are followed by crashes. Heart rate monitoring can help identify when dysautonomia is being triggered.
strategies.notes: Pacing is counterintuitive — it often means doing less than one feels capable of on good days. The goal is stability and gradual expansion of capacity, not rest as an end goal.

strategies.name: Orthostatic intolerance management (fluid and salt loading)
strategies.description: Increasing fluid intake to 2–3 litres per day and sodium intake to 3–5g per day helps expand blood volume and reduce POTS-related symptoms. Compression garments (waist-high, 20–30 mmHg) reduce blood pooling in the lower body. Elevating the head of the bed 10–15° can reduce overnight fluid shifts.
strategies.notes: Salt and fluid loading is not appropriate for individuals with heart failure, hypertension, or renal disease. Should be discussed with a cardiologist or autonomic specialist if POTS is significant.

strategies.name: MCAS management (low-histamine diet, antihistamines, mast cell stabilisers)
strategies.description: For those with comorbid MCAS, a low-histamine diet and avoidance of known mast cell triggers (heat, exercise, fragrances, certain medications) can reduce the frequency and severity of reactions. H1 and H2 antihistamines taken regularly (not just as needed) are often used. Cromolyn sodium (sodium cromoglicate) and quercetin may help stabilise mast cells.
strategies.notes: MCAS management is highly individual — triggers vary widely between people. A food and reaction diary is useful. Work with an allergist or specialist with MCAS knowledge where possible. Elimination diets should not be overly restrictive without dietetic support.

strategies.name: Low-FODMAP or gut-directed dietary approach
strategies.description: For those with IBS-like gut symptoms, a low-FODMAP elimination and reintroduction protocol can identify specific fermentable carbohydrate triggers and reduce bloating, pain, and altered bowel habit. This does not address the underlying motility cause but can significantly reduce symptomatic burden.
strategies.notes: Should be guided by a FODMAP-trained dietitian. Not a long-term diet — reintroduction is essential. May interact with MCAS (some high-FODMAP foods are also high-histamine).

strategies.name: Joint protection and splinting
strategies.description: Soft splints or braces for the most unstable joints (commonly wrists, fingers, knees, and ankles) can reduce subluxation frequency and pain during activities. Occupational therapists can assess and prescribe appropriate supports.
strategies.notes: Splints should not be worn continuously as this can reduce muscle strength and increase dependence. Use for specific tasks. Rigid braces may not be appropriate — EDS-specific assessment matters.

strategies.name: Medications for pain management
strategies.description: Low-dose naltrexone (LDN) has anecdotal and emerging evidence in hEDS/HSD for pain modulation. NSAIDs may help musculoskeletal pain but cause GI problems in a population already prone to them. Gabapentinoids are used for central sensitisation pain but have limited evidence and significant side effects. Tramadol and opioids are generally discouraged. Topical agents (diclofenac gel, lidocaine patches) can be useful for localised pain.
strategies.notes: Pain management in hEDS/HSD requires an individualised approach. Many standard analgesics are poorly tolerated. MCAS may cause reactions to medications (including fillers and dyes). Opioids can worsen GI motility and autonomic function. Discuss with a pain specialist who understands connective tissue conditions.

strategies.name: Psychological support and pain psychology
strategies.description: Acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT) and pain psychology approaches can help individuals adapt to life with chronic pain, reduce suffering, and improve function — without dismissing the physical reality of the condition. Addressing medical trauma and grief is often important.
strategies.notes: Psychological support is not a substitute for physical management. The history of symptoms being dismissed as psychological makes many people understandably reluctant to engage with mental health support. Framing this as brain-body pain science rather than 'it's in your head' is important.

strategies.name: Sleep hygiene and management
strategies.description: Addressing sleep quality through positioning aids (body pillow, supportive mattress), pain management before bed, treating restless leg syndrome if present, and maintaining regular sleep-wake cycles. Melatonin may help circadian dysregulation. Low-dose tricyclic antidepressants are sometimes used for sleep and pain.
strategies.notes: Sleep deprivation significantly worsens pain, autonomic function, and cognitive symptoms. Treatment of comorbid POTS and MCAS often improves sleep quality as a secondary benefit.

strategies.name: Vitamin D, magnesium, and nutritional optimisation
strategies.description: Vitamin D deficiency is common in hEDS/HSD, possibly due to indoor activity limitations and absorption issues. Magnesium plays a role in muscle function and is frequently low. Both may exacerbate pain, fatigue, and muscle cramps when deficient. Routine nutritional assessment is worthwhile.
strategies.notes: Supplementation should follow testing where possible. Magnesium glycinate or malate is often better tolerated in a gut-sensitive population. High-dose supplementation without monitoring is not advisable.

things_to_avoid: Stretching-based exercise (yoga, gymnastics, dance involving extreme range of motion) — increases joint laxity and instability rather than building protective strength
Pushing through severe pain or crashes with 'no pain no gain' mentality — leads to boom-bust cycles and may worsen central sensitisation
Standard physiotherapy that does not account for hypermobility — can cause subluxations and worsen instability
Long periods of complete rest or immobilisation (except acutely) — leads to deconditioning, muscle loss, and worsening joint instability
NSAIDs used long-term — worsen GI symptoms already common in this population and can cause renal and cardiovascular complications
Opioid analgesia as a primary pain management strategy — worsens gut motility, dysautonomia, and has poor long-term efficacy for central sensitisation pain
Highly restrictive elimination diets without dietetic supervision — nutritional deficiency and disordered eating patterns are genuine risks
Accepting diagnoses of 'functional' or 'psychosomatic' symptoms without full evaluation — delays correct diagnosis and appropriate treatment
Ignoring autonomic symptoms or attributing all fatigue to deconditioning — POTS is a real physiological diagnosis requiring specific management

uncomfortable_truths.truth: There is no cure, and the connective tissue defect does not go away. The goal is management, adaptation, and building a sustainable life around the condition — not recovery to a pre-illness baseline.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Many people spend years seeking a diagnosis hoping that once the 'real cause' is found, there will be a treatment that fixes it. The relief of finally having an answer can be followed by grief when it becomes clear that hEDS is lifelong.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who do best with hEDS/HSD typically shift from cure-seeking to function-optimising. They identify their key levers — physiotherapy, POTS management, MCAS control, sleep, pacing — and build a sustainable routine. Quality of life improves significantly even without a cure.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Many mainstream doctors, including rheumatologists and GPs, have limited knowledge of hEDS/HSD and may actively dismiss it. Getting useful care often requires finding the right practitioner, which takes significant effort.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: It is distressing and exhausting to accept that the medical system does not reliably provide good care for this condition. Many people keep hoping the next referral will be to someone who understands.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Those who invest in finding EDS-literate practitioners — including physiotherapists, autonomic specialists, and MCAS-aware allergists — typically receive meaningfully better care. Patient communities and EDS Society practitioner directories are practical starting points.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Exercise is non-negotiable for managing hEDS/HSD, even when it is painful and exhausting. Avoidance leads to deconditioning that makes every symptom worse.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: When movement causes pain, subluxations, and crashes, avoidance feels rational and self-protective. POTS also makes upright exercise feel dangerous. Rest feels safer.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: People who engage in appropriate, paced, strength-focused physiotherapy — even starting with bed-based or pool-based exercise when severely deconditioned — consistently report reduced subluxation frequency, improved pain, and better autonomic function over 6–18 months.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: Psychological symptoms — anxiety, depression, trauma — are real comorbidities in hEDS/HSD that benefit from treatment in their own right, not just as a consequence that will resolve once physical symptoms improve.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: After years of being told that physical symptoms are psychological, people with hEDS/HSD understandably resist any mental health framing. Engaging with psychological care can feel like validating the people who dismissed them.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Addressing trauma, anxiety, and depression through appropriate therapy — particularly from practitioners who understand chronic physical illness — improves pain tolerance, engagement with physical management, and overall quality of life, independently of physical symptom change.

uncomfortable_truths.truth: The 'hypermobility triad' of hEDS + POTS + MCAS means that most people with hEDS have two or three interacting conditions, each of which needs its own management. Treating only one will not produce full improvement.
uncomfortable_truths.why_resisted: Managing three complex conditions simultaneously is overwhelming. It is tempting to focus on one at a time or to hope that treating the 'main' condition will resolve the others.
uncomfortable_truths.path_forward: Practitioners who understand the triad manage all three conditions in parallel. People who get POTS managed alongside hEDS typically find their fatigue, brain fog, and exercise tolerance improve substantially. Adding MCAS management often reduces food reactivity and inflammatory flares.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Muscles are the only controllable stabilisers of hypermobile joints. Building targeted strength — particularly in the hips, core, scapular stabilisers, and feet — reduces subluxations, pain, and the energy cost of movement. This is the highest-impact modifiable intervention available.
key_levers.how_to_start: Find a physiotherapist with documented EDS or hypermobility experience (The Ehlers-Danlos Society has a practitioner directory). Start with an assessment-focused first session, not a treatment session. Expect to begin very slowly — even two exercises two times per week. Pool physiotherapy is an excellent entry point for those who are severely deconditioned.
key_levers.timeline: 3–6 months to notice meaningful change in joint stability; 12–18 months for substantial improvement in function

key_levers.why_it_matters: Unmanaged POTS is responsible for a large proportion of fatigue, cognitive impairment, and exercise intolerance in hEDS/HSD. Addressing it — even with non-pharmacological measures — meaningfully improves daily function and enables participation in physiotherapy.
key_levers.how_to_start: Begin with fluid (2–3L/day) and salt (3–5g/day) loading and compression garments. Track heart rate on standing for a week. Seek referral to cardiology or autonomic neurology if POTS is confirmed or strongly suspected.
key_levers.timeline: Days to weeks for initial symptom reduction from fluid/salt/compression; medication titration may take months

key_levers.lever: Identifying and managing MCAS if present
key_levers.why_it_matters: MCAS drives inflammatory flares, food reactivity, fatigue, and gut symptoms that are otherwise mysterious and hard to manage. Identifying MCAS and implementing basic management (antihistamines, low-histamine diet, trigger avoidance) can produce rapid and significant symptom reduction.
key_levers.how_to_start: Keep a detailed symptom and food diary for 4–6 weeks, looking for reaction patterns. Trial H1 antihistamine (e.g. cetirizine) daily for two weeks and note any improvement. Seek referral to an MCAS-aware allergist or immunologist.
key_levers.timeline: Weeks to months for initial improvement with antihistamines; full trigger identification takes longer

key_levers.why_it_matters: Poor sleep in hEDS/HSD is not just a symptom — it actively worsens pain sensitisation, autonomic dysregulation, and cognitive function. Improving sleep quality creates a positive feedback loop across all other management areas.
key_levers.how_to_start: Assess what is disrupting sleep: pain, restless legs, dysautonomia, anxiety, or positioning. Address each with targeted interventions — body pillow for joint positioning, POTS head-of-bed elevation, pain relief before bed, or sleep hygiene review.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Boom-bust cycles — where individuals overdo activity on good days and crash for multiple days after — are one of the most consistent patterns driving functional decline in hEDS/HSD. Learning to pace within an energy envelope stabilises symptoms and allows gradual capacity expansion.
key_levers.how_to_start: Track activity and symptoms for two weeks to identify current patterns. Identify the activity level that consistently precedes crashes. Set a daily ceiling below that level. Use a heart rate monitor to stay below the anaerobic threshold (approximately 60% of max heart rate for POTS).
key_levers.timeline: Stability within weeks; function expansion over months

key_levers.why_it_matters: hEDS/HSD requires management across multiple body systems. A coordinating clinician — even a well-informed GP — who understands the whole picture significantly reduces the burden of navigating fragmented specialist care.
key_levers.how_to_start: Identify the practitioner in the current care network most willing to coordinate. Prepare a one-page medical summary to share across specialties. Connect with patient communities to find local recommendations for EDS-literate specialists.
key_levers.timeline: Months to build; the investment significantly reduces long-term healthcare frustration

common_mistakes: Seeking a single diagnosis or treatment that explains and fixes everything — hEDS/HSD typically involves multiple interacting conditions, each needing its own management
Resting completely to manage fatigue — leads to deconditioning that makes all symptoms worse and makes physiotherapy harder to begin
Using hypermobility as a feature in yoga, gymnastics, or dance — increases joint laxity and instability, working against stability-focused rehabilitation
Eliminating large food groups without dietetic support — malnutrition, nutrient deficiency, and disordered eating are real risks in a population already prone to gut problems
Trying to manage all symptoms simultaneously without priority — leads to overwhelm and abandonment. POTS management first typically has the greatest downstream benefit
Accepting care from physiotherapists and other practitioners without EDS experience — standard rehabilitation protocols can cause subluxations and worsen instability
Waiting for a formal hEDS diagnosis before beginning management — HSD is managed identically, and the Beighton assessment with an informed physiotherapist is often sufficient to begin rehabilitation
Interpreting improving symptoms as a sign the condition has resolved and stopping management — hEDS/HSD is lifelong, and symptom improvement reflects effective management, not resolution

specialists.role: Primary diagnosing specialist for hEDS/HSD in most healthcare systems. Applies the 2017 diagnostic criteria and rules out other connective tissue disorders. May coordinate with other specialties.
specialists.notes: Many rheumatologists have limited hEDS knowledge. Finding one who lists EDS or hypermobility as an area of interest is important. In some regions, clinical geneticists or sports medicine specialists diagnose more hEDS than rheumatologists.

specialists.role: Rules out other heritable connective tissue disorders (Marfan syndrome, vascular EDS, Loeys-Dietz syndrome) using genetic testing and clinical assessment. Also applies hEDS diagnostic criteria. Often the most knowledgeable about the full EDS spectrum.
specialists.notes: Particularly important if there is a family history, significant skin or vascular features, or concern about vascular EDS. Referral is usually through a specialist.

specialists.role: The single most important therapeutic relationship for most people with hEDS/HSD. Designs and progresses a stability-focused, proprioception-targeted exercise programme appropriate for hypermobile joints. Avoids approaches that worsen laxity.
specialists.notes: Standard physiotherapists without EDS training can cause harm. Ask explicitly whether a physio has experience with hypermobility or EDS before booking. The Ehlers-Danlos Society maintains a directory.

specialists.role: Diagnoses and manages POTS and other dysautonomia features. Can order tilt table testing, prescribe POTS medications (fludrocortisone, midodrine, beta-blockers, ivabradine), and supervise cardiovascular rehabilitation.
specialists.notes: Access is limited in many regions. POTS-specialist cardiologists are more common in tertiary centres. Neurologists with autonomic subspecialty training are the most knowledgeable.

specialists.type: Allergist or immunologist with MCAS experience
specialists.role: Evaluates for mast cell activation syndrome, orders mediator testing, and designs a management protocol including antihistamines, mast cell stabilisers, and trigger management.
specialists.notes: MCAS knowledge among allergists is very uneven. Many mainstream allergists are not familiar with the diagnostic criteria or may be sceptical. Finding one who specifically lists MCAS or mast cell disorders in their practice is important.

specialists.role: Evaluates and manages gut dysmotility, gastroparesis, and related GI complications. Can order gastric emptying studies, oesophageal manometry, and other motility investigations.
specialists.notes: Mainstream gastroenterologists may diagnose IBS without investigating hEDS as a driver. A motility-focused gastroenterologist — ideally one familiar with EDS — is most useful.

specialists.role: Assesses and treats pelvic floor dysfunction, prolapse, bladder and bowel symptoms, and pelvic pain. Essential for many people with hEDS/HSD — particularly women and those with pelvic pain or incontinence.
specialists.notes: Should be experienced with hypermobility-related pelvic floor issues, which differ from standard pelvic floor dysfunction. Both hypertonic (too tight) and hypotonic (too weak) patterns occur in hEDS/HSD.

specialists.role: Manages complex chronic pain including central sensitisation. May use medications (low-dose naltrexone, neuropathic agents), procedural interventions, and interdisciplinary pain programmes.
specialists.notes: Ideally works within a multidisciplinary pain service that includes psychology. Should understand the neurological basis of pain sensitisation in connective tissue disorders.

specialists.role: Provides joint protection education, splinting, adaptive equipment, activity modification, and energy management strategies. Often underutilised but highly practical for daily function.
specialists.notes: OTs with chronic illness experience are most useful. Can assess home and work environments for modifications that reduce injury risk and energy expenditure.

specialists.type: Psychologist or therapist with chronic illness experience
specialists.role: Supports adjustment to chronic illness, addresses medical trauma, provides pain psychology (ACT, CBT-pain), and treats comorbid anxiety and depression.
specialists.notes: Should have experience with chronic physical illness and ideally with EDS or connective tissue conditions. The history of psychological dismissal makes therapeutic alliance particularly important.

patient_communities.name: r/ehlersdanlos
patient_communities.notes: Large, active community with a wide range of experience levels. Useful for finding practitioner recommendations, sharing management strategies, and connecting with others navigating diagnosis. Moderated to maintain quality.

patient_communities.name: The Ehlers-Danlos Society Community
patient_communities.notes: The main international EDS organisation. Provides practitioner directories, educational resources, webinars, and a support community. The website is the best single starting point for newly diagnosed individuals.

patient_communities.name: Hypermobility Connect
patient_communities.notes: Large peer support group for hEDS and HSD. Good for practical day-to-day advice, management tips, and community support from others living with the condition.

patient_communities.name: POTS UK
patient_communities.notes: UK-based resource for POTS and dysautonomia, including management guides, practitioner information, and community support. Valuable for the significant proportion of hEDS/HSD individuals with POTS.

patient_communities.name: Dysautonomia International
patient_communities.notes: Leading organisation for POTS and dysautonomia advocacy, research, and education. Excellent clinical resources and a physician directory for POTS specialists.

patient_communities.name: The Ehlers-Danlos Society — UK
patient_communities.notes: UK-focused EDS charity. Good for UK-specific NHS navigation advice, practitioner finding, and local support groups.

learning_resources.title: The Ehlers-Danlos Society — Patient Resources
learning_resources.description: The authoritative starting point for understanding hEDS and HSD. Includes the full 2017 diagnostic criteria, condition guides, practitioner directories, and research updates. Written for both patients and clinicians.

learning_resources.title: The 2017 International Classification of the Ehlers-Danlos Syndromes
learning_resources.author_or_source: Malfait et al., American Journal of Medical Genetics
learning_resources.description: The definitive diagnostic criteria paper for all EDS subtypes. Useful for understanding exactly how hEDS is diagnosed and why HSD is a separate category. Important reading for those preparing for specialist appointments.

learning_resources.title: Disjointed: Navigating the Diagnosis and Management of Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome and Hypermobility Spectrum Disorders
learning_resources.description: A comprehensive patient-focused guide covering diagnosis, management, comorbidities, and navigating the healthcare system with hEDS/HSD. Written accessibly for those newly diagnosed and those who have struggled to find good care.

learning_resources.title: Hypermobility Syndromes Association (HMSA) — Understanding Hypermobility
learning_resources.description: UK-based organisation with accessible guides on hypermobility, hEDS, and HSD. Particularly useful for understanding the spectrum of hypermobility conditions and finding UK-specific support.

learning_resources.title: Zebra Strong — EDS Physio and Exercise Guide
learning_resources.description: Resources specifically addressing exercise and physiotherapy for hEDS/HSD. Useful for understanding what safe, appropriate exercise looks like for hypermobile joints before finding a specialist physiotherapist.

learning_resources.title: Dysautonomia International — POTS Information
learning_resources.description: Comprehensive guide to POTS — symptoms, diagnosis, and management — essential reading for anyone with hEDS/HSD who suspects autonomic involvement. Includes the non-pharmacological management protocol.

learning_resources.title: Never Bet Against Occam: Mast Cell Activation Disease and the Modern Epidemics of Chronic Illness and Medical Complexity
learning_resources.description: The leading clinician-authored text on MCAS, written accessibly for patients. Covers the history, science, diagnosis, and management of mast cell activation syndrome. Particularly relevant for those with the hEDS-POTS-MCAS triad.

learning_resources.title: EDS and Hypermobility — Pain Neuroscience Education
learning_resources.author_or_source: Lorimer Moseley / various EDS-focused pain scientists
learning_resources.description: Pain neuroscience education resources explaining central sensitisation, how chronic pain changes the nervous system, and why pain in hEDS/HSD is not simply a structural problem. Understanding this supports engagement with psychological and pain management approaches.

learning_resources.title: Chronic Illness and Trauma: Understanding the Connection
learning_resources.author_or_source: Bessel van der Kolk — The Body Keeps the Score
learning_resources.description: While not EDS-specific, this book is widely recommended in the hEDS community for understanding the relationship between trauma, nervous system dysregulation, and chronic physical symptoms. Particularly relevant given the high rates of medical trauma and PTSD in this population.

learning_resources.title: The Muldowney Protocol
learning_resources.description: A structured physiotherapy protocol specifically designed for EDS patients, created by a physiotherapist with extensive hEDS clinical experience. Provides a systematic approach to joint stabilisation exercises. Useful for guiding physiotherapy with practitioners who may be less familiar with hEDS.

sections.relevance_signal: **Joints that bend further than normal**, frequent sprains, stretchy skin. Gut symptoms alongside joint pain, fatigue, and POTS or MCAS. Multi-system involvement.
